metric_name: First computing experience
metric_value: Kastner taught himself FORTRAN at MIT's computer room at 2am in summer 1966 on a $5 million mainframe costing $360/hour to run.
source_page: Opening paragraphs / The Inversion That Keeps Inverting

metric_name: Fundamental compute inversion
metric_value: In 2026 Kastner accesses more compute than a 1966 $5M mainframe from a wrist-worn device running on a battery, for essentially nothing.
notes: Described as 'the most important fact in the history of information technology.'

metric_name: Mainframe CPU hourly cost
metric_value: $360 per hour for compute time on a $5 million mainframe.

metric_name: Scarcity economics of early computing
metric_value: Machine scarce and expensive; human labor abundant and cheap. Every technique — JCL optimization, one-instruction switches, microsecond efficiency — was a response to that economics.
notes: Kastner describes programs as being 'submitted like a prayer' to the machine.

metric_name: Minicomputer democratization onset
metric_value: By the time Kastner left PHI in 1972, minicomputers were democratizing compute access.

metric_name: Compute escaping the glass house
metric_value: By the time Kastner joined Prime in 1979, compute was escaping the glass house.

metric_name: x86 displacement of proprietary platforms
metric_value: By the time Kastner left Stratus in 1987, the x86 architecture was preparing to displace everything that had come before it.

metric_name: Proprietary platform obsolescence timeline
metric_value: By the time Aberdeen was founded in 1988, the question was no longer whether proprietary platforms were doomed but how soon.

metric_name: Generative AI as minicomputer-equivalent disruption
metric_value: Generative AI in 2026 is doing to knowledge work what the minicomputer did to batch processing in the 1970s: making a previously scarce resource suddenly abundant and disruptive.
notes: Impact on workers described as positive and negative.

metric_name: Automation layers preceding full solution
metric_value: The 12-year-old sorting 10,000 pieces of paper would not be surprised that machines eventually handle the paper, but might be surprised by how long it took and how many intermediate layers of partial automation preceded the full solution.

metric_name: Pattern 1: Economic winner displaces technical winner
metric_value: Every major platform transition was driven primarily by economics, not superior engineering. Technical superiority is necessary but not sufficient; technical adequacy plus economic advantage is the sufficient condition.
notes: Named pattern; one of seven recurring structural dynamics Kastner considers most reliable.

metric_name: Mainframe displacement mechanism
metric_value: Mainframe was not technically obsolete when minicomputers displaced it; it was economically uncompetitive for departmental workloads.

metric_name: Minicomputer displacement mechanism
metric_value: Minicomputer was not technically obsolete when x86 servers displaced it; it was economically uncompetitive compared to commodity hardware manufactured at scale.

metric_name: Linux over Unix: licensing economics
metric_value: Unix was not technically inferior to Linux; Linux won on licensing economics and community development.

metric_name: Pattern 2: Proprietary platforms fund their own replacement
metric_value: Proprietary platform achieves leadership through innovation, captures high margins, which attract commodity-based open alternatives that improve rapidly and displace the proprietary platform.
notes: Named pattern; described as 'consistent across every cycle I observed.'

metric_name: IBM funded Unix development
metric_value: IBM funded the research that produced Unix, exemplifying Pattern 2 where proprietary platform margins fund the open alternative that displaces them.

metric_name: Internet boom funded cloud infrastructure
metric_value: The Internet boom funded the cloud infrastructure that commoditized enterprise servers, following Pattern 2.

metric_name: Pattern 3: Adoption timelines always longer than predicted
metric_value: In forty years of making and evaluating technology predictions, Kastner cannot think of a single case where a correct architectural prediction came true on the originally projected timeline.
notes: Named pattern; based on 40-year sample.

metric_name: SOA prediction: directionally right, 5 years early
metric_value: SOA: directionally right, five years early.

metric_name: Open source enterprise adoption: directionally right, 3 years early
metric_value: Open source enterprise adoption: directionally right, three years early.

metric_name: Web as application platform: directionally right, 2 years early
metric_value: Web as application platform: directionally right, two years early.

metric_name: Consumerization of IT: directionally right, 2 years early
metric_value: Consumerization of IT: directionally right, two years early.

metric_name: Organizational friction as adoption bottleneck
metric_value: Technology transitions require organizational competency, risk tolerance, procurement adaptation, skills development, and executive conviction — all moving more slowly than engineering capability.

metric_name: Strategic positioning lead time
metric_value: If you are correct about a technology direction, start positioning three years before you expect mainstream adoption. If you wait until the direction is obvious, the opportunity has passed.
notes: Named practical implication of Pattern 3.

metric_name: AI enterprise adoption timeline
metric_value: AI adoption across large and small enterprises is much more a 2030s story than a 2020s story.
notes: Explicit forward-looking prediction labeled 'Informed Observation' in the text.

metric_name: Pattern 4: The integration problem is permanent
metric_value: Every architectural improvement creates new integration complexity; this is a structural property of technology ecosystems that grow through proliferation rather than replacement.
notes: Named pattern.

metric_name: Integration tax concept coined
metric_value: In the early 1990s Kastner coined 'integration tax' — a permanent overhead cost enterprises pay regardless of how good individual components become.
notes: Named concept originating from Aberdeen Group analysis circa 1992.

metric_name: N-squared point-to-point integration complexity
metric_value: The N-squared complexity of point-to-point integrations was visible and predictable in 1992.

metric_name: Each integration architecture enables new proliferation
metric_value: EAI, SOA, APIs, microservices all addressed integration without eliminating it; each new architecture enabled new application proliferation creating new integration complexity.

metric_name: AI inference integration tax layer
metric_value: As enterprises integrate AI inference engines with operational systems in 2026, the integration tax is being collected again on a new layer. The pattern is unchanged.

metric_name: Integration architecture as first-class infrastructure
metric_value: Enterprises that fared best treated integration architecture as first-class infrastructure from the beginning. Those paying the highest integration tax treated it as an afterthought.

metric_name: Pattern 5: Data advantage compounds — early thesis
metric_value: Kastner argued at Aberdeen in the early 1990s that data was becoming a competitive asset, not merely an operational record.
notes: Named pattern. Described as 'more important than even its most enthusiastic advocates expected.'

metric_name: Data advantage self-reinforcing cycle
metric_value: Early data infrastructure investment produces better analytics, better decisions, better outcomes, more data to analyze. Organizations that defer are in permanent catch-up position.

metric_name: BI data investments as AI foundation
metric_value: Organizations that built large, well-governed data assets in the 1990s-2000s are the same organizations most effectively deploying LLMs and AI inference today. No one planned it that way.

metric_name: IT research archive as data-permanence lesson
metric_value: The Kastner IT research archive would not be possible, nor the bulk of facts in this memoir, without saving copies of digital files including press quotes; data is only permanent with multiple copies in multiple physical locations.

metric_name: Pattern 6: Failure mode is almost never technical
metric_value: In fifty years of watching technology projects succeed and fail, Kastner can count on one hand the projects that failed because of genuinely unsolvable technical problems.
notes: Named pattern.

metric_name: Project failure root causes
metric_value: Overwhelming majority of failures: governance misalignment, inadequate scope definition, requirements changing faster than project accommodated, executive pressure overriding technical judgment, organizational resistance.

metric_name: Technical components vs. human components in failure
metric_value: Technical components can be specified, tested, verified. Human components — politics, incentives, communication failures, misaligned expectations — are significantly harder to manage and much more likely to be source of catastrophic failure.

metric_name: Best architects as organizational diagnosticians
metric_value: The best technology architects Kastner has known were also skilled organizational diagnosticians who could identify political and governance constraints determining whether a sound architecture could be implemented.

metric_name: Pattern 7: The machine was never the hard part
metric_value: Technology problems are tractable. The problems that resisted solution — integration challenges, governance failures, adoption delays, organizational resistance — were human problems requiring human solutions.
notes: Named pattern; described as the conclusion from MIT, PHI, ADL, Prime, Stratus, DEC, Aberdeen, and twenty years of expert witness work.

metric_name: The hard part is knowing what to tell the machine
metric_value: The machine does what it is told, precisely and relentlessly. The hard part is knowing what to tell it.
notes: Closing statement of Pattern 7 section.

metric_name: Kastner's MIT AI environment
metric_value: MIT in 1966 was alive with early AI research energy — LISP, ELIZA, Project MAC, researchers working on foundations of machine learning and knowledge representation.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: Minsky proximity at MIT
metric_value: Kastner's MIT roommate worked around the corner from where Marvin Minsky was building frameworks that defined the AI field for a generation.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: Early AI: explicit knowledge representation limitation
metric_value: Early AI required explicit knowledge representation; fundamental limitation was that human knowledge is mostly tacit — experts know far more than they can articulate.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: Expert systems encoded what experts could say, not what they knew
metric_value: The expert systems of the 1980s encoded what experts could say, not what they actually knew — limited by the knowledge acquisition bottleneck.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: Transformer architecture solved tacit knowledge problem
metric_value: Transformer architecture solved tacit-knowledge limitation through a different approach: training on enough examples of human knowledge in use that patterns emerge implicitly, without explicit rule encoding.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: ELIZA: first chatbot
metric_value: ELIZA (MIT, 1966, Joseph Weizenbaum): first chatbot; demonstrated pattern-matching rules could simulate conversation convincingly. No actual language understanding; worked by reflecting user statements as questions.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: ELIZA Turing Test implications
metric_value: ELIZA fooled users who knew it was a program; Turing Test implications were described as 'unsettling.'
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: Expert systems: commercially significant but brittle
metric_value: Expert systems (1980s): commercially significant but limited by knowledge acquisition bottleneck. Encoding human expertise was slow, expensive, and produced brittle systems that failed outside narrow design domain.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: MYCIN: medical diagnosis expert system
metric_value: MYCIN: rule-based medical diagnosis system; cited as representative commercially significant expert system of the 1980s.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: XCON: computer configuration expert system
metric_value: XCON: rule-based computer configuration expert system for DEC; cited as representative commercially significant expert system of the 1980s.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: 1990s machine learning: transition from rules to statistics
metric_value: 1990s ML: statistical approaches (decision trees, neural networks, SVMs) learned patterns from data rather than explicit rules. Required labeled training data; worked on specific well-defined tasks.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: 2000s-2010s deep learning enabled by GPU
metric_value: Deep learning: multi-layer neural networks enabled by GPU computing and large digital datasets. Applications: image recognition, speech recognition, recommendation systems.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: AlexNet 2012 breakthrough
metric_value: AlexNet (2012) demonstrated that deep learning could outperform human-engineered features on image classification.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: Transformer paper published
metric_value: "Attention is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., Google, 2017): self-attention mechanisms for parallel sequence processing; enabled training on vastly larger datasets efficiently.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: LLM generation: emergent capabilities
metric_value: LLMs (2020-2026): GPT, Claude, Gemini; trained on internet-scale text; demonstrate emergent capabilities not explicitly trained at sufficient scale.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: Six decades of compounding AI progress
metric_value: The thread from ELIZA to GPT-4 is six decades of compounding progress. Each generation solved problems the previous could not while introducing new limitations the next would address.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: LLMs: language fluency combined with broad knowledge
metric_value: What makes current AI generation genuinely different: combination of language fluency with broad knowledge and reasoning capability. Previous AI systems were narrow; LLMs are broad general-purpose cognitive tools.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026
notes: Kastner explicitly notes he has heard 'this time is different' many times.

metric_name: Narrow vs. broad AI: economic calculus
metric_value: A narrow AI system automates one task. A broad AI system augments one worker across many tasks. The economic calculus is different in kind, not just in degree.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: 2026 inversion: machine inference abundant, human judgment scarce
metric_value: In 2026: machine inference abundant and cheap; human judgment and creativity the scarce resource. Jobs resisting automation are those requiring contextual judgment, relationship management, navigating ambiguity.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026
notes: Framed as 'the 1969 inversion in reverse.'

metric_name: Got right: open systems transition
metric_value: Open systems transition correctly predicted.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Got right: RDBMS dominance for operational systems
metric_value: Dominance of RDBMS for operational systems correctly predicted.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Got right: compounding value of data
metric_value: Compounding value of data correctly predicted.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Got right: permanence of integration problem
metric_value: Permanence of the integration problem correctly predicted.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Got right: proprietary platforms funding displacement
metric_value: Pattern of proprietary platforms funding their own displacement correctly identified.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Got right: consumerization of IT
metric_value: Consumerization of IT correctly predicted.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Got right: broad trajectory of AI
metric_value: Broad trajectory of AI from narrow tools toward general-purpose cognitive capability correctly predicted.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Missed: object databases
metric_value: Object databases: a miss. They did not achieve the predicted market significance.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Missed: Linux path to Unix dominance
metric_value: Linux path to Unix dominance: right destination, wrong mechanism.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Missed: speed of web as full application platform
metric_value: Web transformation into full application platform: got direction right but missed speed of transition.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Missed: public cloud as dominant infrastructure model
metric_value: Emergence of public cloud as dominant enterprise infrastructure: understood compute commoditization but did not anticipate infrastructure migrating to shared utility services managed by Amazon, Microsoft, Google rather than enterprise-owned commodity hardware.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Expected cloud winners were wrong
metric_value: Cloud infrastructure failed to come from established vendors like Dell, HP, and IBM as Kastner expected.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Biggest miss: smartphone enterprise transformation
metric_value: Biggest miss: underestimated how thoroughly the smartphone would transform enterprise IT. Consumerization thesis was correct but confined to UI expectations; missed deeper architecture implications of mobile-first.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed
notes: Kastner specifically notes that in 2005 he did not recognize the full architecture implications.

metric_name: Mobile-first vs. mobile-compatible architecture distinction
metric_value: A billion people carrying always-connected computers changed what enterprise applications needed to do, not just how they needed to look. Mobile-first, not just mobile-compatible, had larger architecture implications.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: Mobile transactions-per-second scale
metric_value: The transactions-per-second numbers this decade are mind-boggling.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed
notes: Qualitative characterization without specific figure.

metric_name: Systematic analytical bias: direction right, speed wrong
metric_value: All misses share a common structure: correctly identified direction of change but underestimated speed and completeness of transition.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed / Systematic bias

metric_name: Technically sophisticated analyst's conservative bias
metric_value: Analysts who understand technology deeply tend to be conservative about adoption timelines because they understand organizational friction in detail — right about friction, wrong about endpoint.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed / Systematic bias

metric_name: Bold directional analyst vs. friction-aware analyst
metric_value: Analyst who ignores friction and calls direction boldly is often right about endpoint, wrong about timeline. Best prediction: confident direction with realistic timeline — harder to produce and easier to claim in retrospect.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed / Systematic bias

metric_name: Specialization creates more defensible value than breadth
metric_value: Firms that maintained premium pricing across technology cycles had genuine depth in specific domains. Breadth is easier to approximate; depth requires investment and time that cannot be easily replicated.

metric_name: Cooperative economics prevent fragmentation
metric_value: Firms built on eat-what-you-kill models fragment when successful rainmakers go independent. Multi-layer revenue sharing (Aberdeen model) created genuine financial interdependency and incentive to help each other succeed.

metric_name: Technical credibility opens different doors
metric_value: Engagements creating most value were where Aberdeen's technical depth allowed conversations market-analysis firms could not have. An analyst who can identify specific DB performance problems commands different pricing than one who describes market trends.

metric_name: Culture set in first three years
metric_value: Everything Aberdeen was and was not after 1997 traces back to what the founding partnership established between 1990 and 1993. Culture change after scale is orders of magnitude harder than culture formation before scale.

metric_name: Aberdeen publishing model anticipated content marketing by 15 years
metric_value: Free research as marketing anticipated content marketing by fifteen years. Principle: demonstrating expertise before the sales conversation produces better clients. Distribution mechanism changed; principle has not.
notes: Aberdeen founded 1988; if content marketing mainstreamed ~2003-2005, the 15-year claim is internally consistent.

metric_name: Origin of automation conviction
metric_value: A 12-year-old sorting 10,000 pieces of paper on Cape Cod was convinced machines should handle this so people could do something better with their time. The question was correct; the timeline longer than expected.
notes: Kastner was approximately 12 in the mid-1950s given a 2026 memoir covering 60 years from approximately 1966.

metric_name: Automation progression stages
metric_value: The answer arrived in stages: punch-card tabulation, batch processing, OLTP, relational databases, client-server, the web, mobile computing, cloud infrastructure, AI inference.

metric_name: Federal Reserve check-sorting workforce 1960
metric_value: The Federal Reserve Bank employed half a million people to sort checks every night in 1960; now employs a small fraction for vastly larger transaction volumes.
notes: Specific figure (half a million) is a rhetorical claim; no external source cited by Kastner.

metric_name: Restaurant bookkeeping automation
metric_value: Restaurant bookkeeping department that required three people through a summer season to reconcile 10,000 pieces of paper can now be automated by a POS system on a tablet in a hostess stand.
notes: References the Cape Cod bookkeeping origin story from earlier chapters.

metric_name: AI handling the next layer of paper
metric_value: AI inference is handling the next layer of paper — summarization, pattern recognition, first-draft generation, routine analysis. The question is: what should we do with the time automation frees?

metric_name: Automation raises harder question than automation itself
metric_value: What humans should do with the time automation freed turns out to be harder than the automation question. It requires judgment, values, social structures, and economic arrangements that technology alone cannot provide.

metric_name: IT industry 1966-2026: dual legacy
metric_value: IT industry created more economic value, social transformation, and improvement in human capability than any other industry over a comparable 60-year span — and also immense waste, broken promises, vendor lock-in, failed projects, security failures, privacy violations.
source_page: A Final Observation on the Industry I Loved

metric_name: Industry actors worked with incomplete information and justified optimism
metric_value: People inside the industry worked with incomplete information, real disagreement, and more optimism than the evidence strictly justified. Most of the time, that optimism was warranted. Sometimes it was not. The balance was sufficient.
source_page: A Final Observation on the Industry I Loved

metric_name: Career vantage points
metric_value: Kastner saw the industry from machine room, consulting engagement, product war room, executive suite, and analyst firm across fifty years.
source_page: A Final Observation on the Industry I Loved

metric_name: The machine is the whole system
metric_value: The machine has never been only the hardware, software, or network. The machine is the whole system: code, economics, institutions, incentives, ambitions, and the human beings who must make it work.
source_page: A Final Observation on the Industry I Loved
notes: Central thesis of the memoir.

metric_name: Core wisdom: progress and waste are both real
metric_value: To spend a life inside the machine is to learn that progress is real, waste is real, and wisdom lies in telling the difference.
source_page: A Final Observation on the Industry I Loved
notes: Closing statement of the chapter.

metric_name: Fifty years: small number of recurring structural dynamics
metric_value: Technology markets follow a small number of structural dynamics that recur across platforms, cycles, and decades. Kastner identifies seven as most reliable.
notes: Chapter's central structural claim.

metric_name: AI impacted jobs: highest-context, not highest-education
metric_value: Jobs resisting automation are not those requiring most education or most physical skill — they are those requiring most contextual judgment, relationship management, and ability to navigate ambiguity with incomplete information.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: Human solutions required for human problems
metric_value: Alignment of incentives, clarity of communication, organizational culture supporting rather than resisting change — these are the solutions to the problems technology cannot solve.

metric_name: Expert witness career duration
metric_value: Twenty years of expert witness work is listed as one of Kastner's career vantage points contributing to his conclusion that the machine was never the hard part.

metric_name: LLMs as first genuinely broad general-purpose cognitive tools
metric_value: LLMs are the first AI systems capable enough across wide range of knowledge-work tasks to be genuinely useful as general-purpose cognitive tools — not just narrow automation.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026
notes: Prescience: high — stated at time of initial broad availability, consistent with subsequent enterprise adoption patterns.

metric_name: Memoir argument: the machine is the whole system
metric_value: If this book has an argument, it is that the machine has never been only hardware, software, or network. The machine is the whole system: code, economics, institutions, incentives, ambitions, and human beings.
source_page: A Final Observation on the Industry I Loved
notes: Explicit thesis statement of the entire memoir.

metric_name: Question without technical answer
metric_value: The question of what humans should do with time freed by automation does not have a technical answer. It never did.

metric_name: SOA was directionally correct, premature
metric_value: SOA: directionally right, five years early — characteristic of the systematic analytical bias of correctly identifying direction but underestimating adoption friction.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed / Seven Patterns That Repeat

metric_name: Aberdeen founding year
metric_value: Aberdeen Group founded 1988; culture set between 1990 and 1993 in the founding partnership years.

metric_name: Aberdeen culture locked in by 1997
metric_value: Everything Aberdeen was and was not after 1997 traces to what founding partnership established 1990-1993.

metric_name: Content marketing principle durable; delivery mechanism changed
metric_value: Mechanism for distributing research has changed entirely (direct mail and early websites to search, social, AI-mediated discovery). The underlying principle — demonstrate expertise before the sales conversation — has not.

metric_name: JCL optimization as scarcity-driven technique
metric_value: JCL optimization, one-instruction switches, microsecond-level efficiency were all responses to scarce, expensive compute economics — techniques that became obsolete as the inversion proceeded.

metric_name: LLM breadth changes economic calculus
metric_value: Breadth of LLMs changes the economics: augments one worker across many tasks rather than automating one task. Different in kind, not just degree.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: Data as competitive asset thesis — Aberdeen origin
metric_value: Kastner argued at Aberdeen in the early 1990s that data was becoming a competitive asset, not merely an operational record — proven more important than even its most enthusiastic advocates expected.

metric_name: GPU computing as deep learning enabler
metric_value: Deep learning was enabled by GPU computing and large digital datasets — without GPU hardware, the mathematical approaches that became deep learning could not have scaled.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: Self-attention enabling parallel sequence processing
metric_value: Self-attention mechanisms could process entire sequences in parallel rather than sequentially — the architectural innovation enabling training on vastly larger datasets efficiently.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: Transition from rules to statistics in AI
metric_value: 1990s ML represented the key architectural transition in AI: from explicit rules to statistical patterns learned from labeled data.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: LLMs demonstrate emergent capabilities at scale
metric_value: LLMs demonstrate emergent capabilities — skills not explicitly trained — at sufficient scale. First AI systems to handle ambiguity, context, and implicit knowledge qualitatively differently from predecessors.
source_page: AI Sidebar: From ELIZA to GPT

metric_name: Aggregated memoir sources and analysis
metric_value: Commentary on fifty years matters; the author brings perspective as programmer, consultant, marketer, researcher, expert witness, and co-founder across multiple platform transitions.

metric_name: IT industry created more economic value than any comparable 60-year industry
metric_value: Information technology industry 1966-2026 created more economic value, social transformation, and genuine improvement in everyday human capability than any other industry over a comparable sixty-year span.
source_page: A Final Observation on the Industry I Loved
notes: Superlative claim without quantification; author's qualitative assessment.

metric_name: Both columns: IT progress and IT damage are true simultaneously
metric_value: IT created immense waste, broken promises, vendor lock-in, failed projects, security failures, privacy violations, and disruptions imposed on millions. Both progress and damage are true; neither cancels the other.
source_page: A Final Observation on the Industry I Loved

metric_name: Cloud utility model vs. enterprise-owned commodity hardware
metric_value: Infrastructure migrated to shared utility services managed by Amazon, Microsoft, Google — not to enterprise-owned commodity hardware as would have been a reasonable extrapolation of earlier trends.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed

metric_name: BI data investments of 1990s are AI foundational assets of 2020s
metric_value: Data investments made for business intelligence thirty years ago are foundational assets for AI capability today. No one planned it that way. But the architecture was right.

metric_name: Data permanence requires multiple copies in multiple locations
metric_value: Data is only permanent if you have multiple copies in multiple physical and accessible locations.
notes: Personal lesson from building the Kastner IT research archive.

metric_name: Generative AI impact on knowledge workers: positive and negative
metric_value: The impact of generative AI on workers is positive and negative — the same pattern as prior compute-democratization waves.

metric_name: New proprietary platform success contains seeds of displacement
metric_value: A new proprietary platform's success contains the seeds of its eventual displacement — the pattern is so reliable it is structural.

metric_name: Proprietary minicomputer margins funded x86 displacement
metric_value: Proprietary minicomputer margins funded the x86 development that displaced minicomputers — a direct illustration of Pattern 2.

metric_name: LISP as early AI research language at MIT
metric_value: LISP was part of MIT's early AI research environment in 1966, alongside ELIZA and Project MAC.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: Project MAC as early AI/timesharing hub
metric_value: MIT's Project MAC was active in 1966 and part of the early AI research energy that Kastner encountered as a student.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026

metric_name: Client-server as automation layer in progression
metric_value: Client-server architecture is one of the intermediate layers in the automation progression from punch cards to AI inference.

metric_name: OLTP as automation layer
metric_value: Online transaction processing is one of the intermediate automation layers between early batch computing and modern AI inference.

metric_name: Web as automation layer in progression
metric_value: The web is one of the intermediate automation layers in the progression from batch processing to AI inference.

metric_name: Relational databases as automation layer
metric_value: Relational databases are one of the intermediate automation layers in the progression from batch computing to AI inference.

metric_name: Mobile computing as automation layer
metric_value: Mobile computing is one of the intermediate automation layers preceding AI inference in the full automation progression.

metric_name: Cloud infrastructure as automation layer
metric_value: Cloud infrastructure is one of the intermediate automation layers preceding AI inference in the full automation progression.

metric_name: Career roles enumerated
metric_value: Kastner's career roles: programmer, consultant, marketer, researcher, expert witness, co-founder — spanning 1966 to 2026.

metric_name: Technical sophistication and conservative adoption bias — permanent tension
metric_value: There is a permanent tension in technology analysis between the friction-aware analyst (right about friction, wrong about endpoint) and the directional analyst (right about endpoint, wrong about timeline).
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed / Systematic bias

metric_name: Bold bets on correct direction more profitable than detailed friction analysis
metric_value: Organizations that made the most money in technology transitions often bet more aggressively on the direction being right than the detailed analysis of friction suggested was rational.
source_page: What I Got Right and What I Missed / Systematic bias

metric_name: AI as next inversion: inference abundant, judgment scarce
metric_value: The same economic inversion that replaced expensive scarce compute with cheap abundant compute is now replacing expensive knowledge-work tasks with cheap AI inference, making human contextual judgment the new scarce resource.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026
notes: High prescience: stated at cusp of generative AI mainstream adoption.

metric_name: Breadth of LLMs is genuinely different this time
metric_value: This time is different: previous 'different' claims referred to narrow AI. LLM breadth across many knowledge-work tasks is qualitatively new.
source_page: On Artificial Intelligence: The View from 2026
